revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[zh:乐山堂]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[zh:乐山堂]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[zh:乐山堂]]



{{Not|Longshou Temple}}
{{Coord|27|05|58.6|N|119|54|49.4|E|display=title}}
[[File:泗洲文佛像和乐山堂遗址.jpg|thumb|Photo of the Leshan Hall ruins exhibited at the {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County}} Museum]]

'''Leshan Hall''', commonly known as '''Gaizhu Hall''', formerly known as '''Longshou Temple''', is located in China, {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian Province}}, {{ill|Ningde|en|Ningde|WD=Q68919|lt=Ningde City}}, {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County}}, {{ill|Baiyang Township|de|Baiyang Township|ja|柏洋郷|zh|柏洋乡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baiyang Township|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|de|Tahu-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|塔後村 (霞浦県)|zh|塔后村|lt=Tahu Village|lt_ja=塔後村|lt_de=Tahu-Dorf|lt_zh=塔后村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|de|Shangwan-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|上万村 (霞浦県)|zh|上万村|lt=Shangwan Natural Village|lt_ja=上万自然村|lt_de=Shangwan-Naturdorf|lt_zh=上万自然村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was established by Fujian {{ill|Mingjiao|de|Mingjiao|ja|明教|zh|明教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mingjiao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐代三夷教|1=Three Persian religions in Tang dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418516}} ({{ill|Manichaeism|en|Manichaeism|WD=Q131165|lt=Manichaeism}}) follower {{ill|Sun Mian|de|Sun Mian|ja|孫綿|zh|孙绵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Mian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} during the {{ill|Northern Song|de|Nördliche Song-Dynastie|ja|北宋|zh|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} period in the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD), making it the earliest Manichaean building constructed in Fujian. For over a thousand years, it served as an important venue for Manichaean activities in the Baiyang area until it was destroyed in 2006 by {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Sangmei Typhoon|lt_ja=桑美台風|lt_de=Sangmei-Taifun|lt_zh=桑美台风|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |author=樊丽沙 |author2=杨富学 |title=霞浦摩尼教文献及其重要性 |journal=世界宗教研究 |language=zh |publisher=中国社会科学院世界宗教研究所 |publication-place=北京 |publication-date=2011 |issue=06 |issn=1000-4289}}</ref>
<ref name="Kósa2018">{{Cite book|last=Kósa|first=Gábor|title=Der östliche Manichäismus im Spiegel seiner Buch- und Schriftkultur|publisher=Walter de Gruyter GmbH & Co KG|date=2018|isbn=978-3-11-059393-8|language=de}}</ref>

== Name ==
Leshan Hall was originally established as "Longshou Temple" and was renamed "Leshan Hall" during the Yuan dynasty. Initially, the name "Leshan" was believed to be derived from the phrase "The wise delight in water, and the benevolent delight in mountains" found in {{ill|Analects|en|Analects|WD=Q276015|lt=The Analects}} (Lunyu: Yong Ye). Another theory suggests that the term "Leshan" originates from {{ill|Middle Persian|de|Mittelpersisch|ja|中古ペルシア語|zh|中古波斯语|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Persian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{Lang|pal|Rōšan}} (or {{Lang|pal|Rōšn}}), meaning "light", which corresponds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light. Moreover, since the building was more likely used as a dining hall for followers rather than as a monastery (see the "Architectural Style and Nature" section), the change from "Temple" (寺) to "Hall" (堂) was probably due to this reason.
<ref name=":0" />

== Historical Development ==
According to the explicit records in the "Sun Family Genealogy of the Republic of China" compiled in the 21st year of the Republic (1932), Sun Mian from Chanyang Village (now also known as Shenyang Village, under the jurisdiction of Baiyang Township) purchased the land during the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD) and commenced the construction of Longshou Temple, becoming its first abbot. In the fifth year of {{ill|Tiansheng|de|Tiansheng|ja|天聖|zh|天圣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tiansheng|1=Tiansheng|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939230}} (1027 AD), a resident of Shangwan Village, {{ill|Lin Deng|de|Lin Deng|ja|林瞪|zh|林瞪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lin Deng|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, converted to Mingjiao at Longshou Temple, later becoming the leading missionary master of Mingjiao in the area and the second abbot of Longshou Temple. His direct disciple, Chen Pingshan, succeeded him.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=杨富学 |title=林瞪及其在中国摩尼教史上的地位 |journal=中国史研究 |language=zh |publication-place=北京市 |publication-date=2014 |issue=01 |page=109-124 |issn=1002-7963}}</ref>

During the Yuan dynasty, Longshou Temple was renamed Leshan Hall, and during the Ming and Qing dynasties, Leshan Hall underwent two major renovations. In the early years of the People's Republic of China, according to local elders, Leshan Hall still had several courtyards. At the beginning of the {{ill|Cultural Revolution|en|Cultural Revolution|WD=Q8690|lt=Cultural Revolution}}, Leshan Hall was demolished and subsequently received only simple repairs with makeshift shed-like structures used for storing villagers' farming tools and for temporary shelter from rain. In 2006, Leshan Hall was destroyed by the {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Super Typhoon Sangmei|lt_ja=超強台風桑美|lt_de=Super-Taifun Sangmei|lt_zh=超强台风桑美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leaving only the foundation columns, steps, and the base of the shrine.
<ref name=":0" />

== Architectural Style and Nature ==
Due to its great antiquity and the lack of written records, the original appearance of Leshan Hall is unknown. The site covers an area of approximately 1560 square meters. Based on photographs of the makeshift structure built at the end of the Cultural Revolution, Leshan Hall relied on columns for support and had no walls on any side. If this construction method was similar to the original, then Leshan Hall would not have been suitable for monastic practice; furthermore, its location at the village entrance, where followers tended to gather, rather than in a secluded area suitable for monks, indicates that Leshan Hall did not possess the typical characteristics of a {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=Temple}}, but rather those of a dining hall. This is one possible reason for the renaming from Longshou Temple to Leshan Hall.
<ref name=":0" />

== Related Activities ==
Every year on the {{ill|Ghost Festival|de|Geisterfest|ja|中元節|zh|中元节|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ghost Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ulambana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}} (the fifteenth day of the seventh month of the {{ill|Lunar Calendar|de|Mondkalender|ja|農暦|zh|农历|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国暦|1=Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134032}}), the Manichaean believers of Shangwan Village gather at Leshan Hall to perform a ritual for the transference of the souls of the deceased. This concept of soul transference is at odds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light, indicating that Manichaeism in Xiapu, in order to become popular and endure locally, had incorporated some unique elements—such as this soul-transference ritual—that provided comfort to the people.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=马小鹤 |author2=吴春明 |title=摩尼教与济度亡灵——霞浦明教《奏申牒书科册》研究 |journal=九州学林 |language=zh |publication-date=2010 |issue=03 |page=45-46}}</ref>

  A collection of important documents on Manichaeism in Xiapu, it is currently the longest extant collection of Manichaean Chinese texts, with an account of Leshan Hall found in the manuscript "Leshan Hall Divine Record".
* {{ill|Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|de|Manichäische Steinskulpturen aus Shangwan Village|ja|上万村摩尼教石彫造像|zh|上万村摩尼教石雕造像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Built near Leshan Hall to adorn the Three Buddha Pagoda behind the tower and the Dengdong Stone Pagoda, it is one of the few surviving examples of Manichaean stone carving art.
* {{ill|Fushou Palace in Puxi|de|Fushou-Palast in Puxi|ja|浦西境福寿宮|zh|浦西境福寿宫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushou Palace in Puxi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Located outside the south gate of {{ill|Fuzhou|en|Fuzhou|WD=Q68481|lt=Fuzhou City}}, it is the best-preserved Manichaean palace building, enshrining Lin Deng who once aided in disaster relief in Fuzhou.

== Historical Development ==
According to the explicit records in the "Sun Family Genealogy of the Republic of China" compiled in the 21st year of the Republic (1932), Sun Mian from Chanyang Village (now also known as Shenyang Village, under the jurisdiction of Baiyang Township) purchased the land during the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD) and commenced the construction of Longshou Temple, becoming its first abbot. In the fifth year of {{ill|Tiansheng|de|Tiansheng|ja|天聖|zh|天圣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tiansheng|1=Tiansheng|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939230}} (1027 AD), a resident of Shangwan Village, {{ill|Lin Deng|de|Lin Deng|ja|林瞪|zh|林瞪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lin Deng|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, converted to Mingjiao at Longshou Temple, later becoming the leading missionary master of Mingjiao in the area and the second abbot of Longshou Temple. His direct disciple, Chen Pingshan, succeeded him.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=杨富学 |title=林瞪及其在中国摩尼教史上的地位 |journal=中国史研究 |language=zh |publication-place=北京市 |publication-date=2014 |issue=01 |page=109-124 |issn=1002-7963}}</ref>

During the Yuan dynasty, Longshou Temple was renamed Leshan Hall, and during the Ming and Qing dynasties, Leshan Hall underwent two major renovations. In the early years of the People's Republic of China, according to local elders, Leshan Hall still had several courtyards. At the beginning of the {{ill|Cultural Revolution|en|Cultural Revolution|WD=Q8690|lt=Cultural Revolution}}, Leshan Hall was demolished and subsequently received only simple repairs with makeshift shed-like structures used for storing villagers' farming tools and for temporary shelter from rain. In 2006, Leshan Hall was destroyed by the {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Super Typhoon Sangmei|lt_ja=超強台風桑美|lt_de=Super-Taifun Sangmei|lt_zh=超强台风桑美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leaving only the foundation columns, steps, and the base of the shrine.
<ref name=":0" />

== Architectural Style and Nature ==
Due to its great antiquity and the lack of written records, the original appearance of Leshan Hall is unknown. The site covers an area of approximately 1560 square meters. Based on photographs of the makeshift structure built at the end of the Cultural Revolution, Leshan Hall relied on columns for support and had no walls on any side. If this construction method was similar to the original, then Leshan Hall would not have been suitable for monastic practice; furthermore, its location at the village entrance, where followers tended to gather, rather than in a secluded area suitable for monks, indicates that Leshan Hall did not possess the typical characteristics of a {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=Temple}}, but rather those of a dining hall. This is one possible reason for the renaming from Longshou Temple to Leshan Hall.
<ref name=":0" />

== Related Activities ==
Every year on the {{ill|Ghost Festival|de|Geisterfest|ja|中元節|zh|中元节|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ghost Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ulambana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}} (the fifteenth day of the seventh month of the {{ill|Lunar Calendar|de|Mondkalender|ja|農暦|zh|农历|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国暦|1=Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134032}}), the Manichaean believers of Shangwan Village gather at Leshan Hall to perform a ritual for the transference of the souls of the deceased. This concept of soul transference is at odds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light, indicating that Manichaeism in Xiapu, in order to become popular and endure locally, had incorporated some unique elements—such as this soul-transference ritual—that provided comfort to the people.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=马小鹤 |author2=吴春明 |title=摩尼教与济度亡灵——霞浦明教《奏申牒书科册》研究 |journal=九州学林 |language=zh |publication-date=2010 |issue=03 |page=45-46}}</ref>

== See Also ==
* {{ill|Xiapu Documents|de|Xiapu-Dokumente|ja|霞浦文書|zh|霞浦文书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiapu Documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  A collection of important documents on Manichaeism in Xiapu, it is currently the longest extant collection of Manichaean Chinese texts, with an account of Leshan Hall found in the manuscript "Leshan Hall Divine Record".
* {{ill|Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|de|Manichäische Steinskulpturen aus Shangwan Village|ja|上万村摩尼教石彫造像|zh|上万村摩尼教石雕造像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Built near Leshan Hall to adorn the Three Buddha Pagoda behind the tower and the Dengdong Stone Pagoda, it is one of the few surviving examples of Manichaean stone carving art.
* {{ill|Fushou Palace in Puxi|de|Fushou-Palast in Puxi|ja|浦西境福寿宮|zh|浦西境福寿宫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushou Palace in Puxi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Located outside the south gate of {{ill|Fuzhou|en|Fuzhou|WD=Q68481|lt=Fuzhou City}}, it is the best-preserved Manichaean palace building, enshrining Lin Deng who once aided in disaster relief in Fuzhou.

{{Not|Longshou Temple}}
{{Coord|27|05|58.6|N|119|54|49.4|E|display=title}}
[[File:泗洲文佛像和乐山堂遗址.jpg|thumb|Photo of the Leshan Hall ruins exhibited at the {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County}} Museum]]

'''Leshan Hall''', commonly known as '''Gaizhu Hall''', formerly known as '''Longshou Temple''', is located in China, {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian Province}}, {{ill|Ningde|en|Ningde|WD=Q68919|lt=Ningde City}}, {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County}}, {{ill|Baiyang Township|de|Baiyang Township|ja|柏洋郷|zh|柏洋乡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baiyang Township|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|de|Tahu-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|塔後村 (霞浦県)|zh|塔后村|lt=Tahu Village|lt_ja=塔後村|lt_de=Tahu-Dorf|lt_zh=塔后村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|de|Shangwan-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|上万村 (霞浦県)|zh|上万村|lt=Shangwan Natural Village|lt_ja=上万自然村|lt_de=Shangwan-Naturdorf|lt_zh=上万自然村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was established by Fujian {{ill|Mingjiao|de|Mingjiao|ja|明教|zh|明教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mingjiao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐代三夷教|1=Three Persian religions in Tang dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418516}} ({{ill|Manichaeism|en|Manichaeism|WD=Q131165|lt=Manichaeism}}) follower {{ill|Sun Mian|de|Sun Mian|ja|孫綿|zh|孙绵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Mian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} during the {{ill|Northern Song|de|Nördliche Song-Dynastie|ja|北宋|zh|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} period in the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD), making it the earliest Manichaean building constructed in Fujian. For over a thousand years, it served as an important venue for Manichaean activities in the Baiyang area until it was destroyed in 2006 by {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Sangmei Typhoon|lt_ja=桑美台風|lt_de=Sangmei-Taifun|lt_zh=桑美台风|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |author=樊丽沙 |author2=杨富学 |title=霞浦摩尼教文献及其重要性 |journal=世界宗教研究 |language=zh |publisher=中国社会科学院世界宗教研究所 |publication-place=北京 |publication-date=2011 |issue=06 |issn=1000-4289}}</ref>
<ref name="Kósa2018">{{Cite book|last=Kósa|first=Gábor|title=Der östliche Manichäismus im Spiegel seiner Buch- und Schriftkultur|publisher=Walter de Gruyter GmbH & Co KG|date=2018|isbn=978-3-11-059393-8|language=de}}</ref>

== Name ==
Leshan Hall was originally established as "Longshou Temple" and was renamed "Leshan Hall" during the Yuan dynasty. Initially, the name "Leshan" was believed to be derived from the phrase "The wise delight in water, and the benevolent delight in mountains" found in {{ill|Analects|en|Analects|WD=Q276015|lt=The Analects}} (Lunyu: Yong Ye). Another theory suggests that the term "Leshan" originates from {{ill|Middle Persian|de|Mittelpersisch|ja|中古ペルシア語|zh|中古波斯语|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Persian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{Lang|pal|Rōšan}} (or {{Lang|pal|Rōšn}}), meaning "light", which corresponds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light. Moreover, since the building was more likely used as a dining hall for followers rather than as a monastery (see the "Architectural Style and Nature" section), the change from "Temple" (寺) to "Hall" (堂) was probably due to this reason.
<ref name=":0" />

== Historical Development ==
According to the explicit records in the "Sun Family Genealogy of the Republic of China" compiled in the 21st year of the Republic (1932), Sun Mian from Chanyang Village (now also known as Shenyang Village, under the jurisdiction of Baiyang Township) purchased the land during the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD) and commenced the construction of Longshou Temple, becoming its first abbot. In the fifth year of {{ill|Tiansheng|de|Tiansheng|ja|天聖|zh|天圣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tiansheng|1=Tiansheng|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939230}} (1027 AD), a resident of Shangwan Village, {{ill|Lin Deng|de|Lin Deng|ja|林瞪|zh|林瞪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lin Deng|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, converted to Mingjiao at Longshou Temple, later becoming the leading missionary master of Mingjiao in the area and the second abbot of Longshou Temple. His direct disciple, Chen Pingshan, succeeded him.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=杨富学 |title=林瞪及其在中国摩尼教史上的地位 |journal=中国史研究 |language=zh |publication-place=北京市 |publication-date=2014 |issue=01 |page=109-124 |issn=1002-7963}}</ref>

During the Yuan dynasty, Longshou Temple was renamed Leshan Hall, and during the Ming and Qing dynasties, Leshan Hall underwent two major renovations. In the early years of the People's Republic of China, according to local elders, Leshan Hall still had several courtyards. At the beginning of the {{ill|Cultural Revolution|en|Cultural Revolution|WD=Q8690|lt=Cultural Revolution}}, Leshan Hall was demolished and subsequently received only simple repairs with makeshift shed-like structures used for storing villagers' farming tools and for temporary shelter from rain. In 2006, Leshan Hall was destroyed by the {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Super Typhoon Sangmei|lt_ja=超強台風桑美|lt_de=Super-Taifun Sangmei|lt_zh=超强台风桑美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leaving only the foundation columns, steps, and the base of the shrine.
<ref name=":0" />

== Architectural Style and Nature ==
Due to its great antiquity and the lack of written records, the original appearance of Leshan Hall is unknown. The site covers an area of approximately 1560 square meters. Based on photographs of the makeshift structure built at the end of the Cultural Revolution, Leshan Hall relied on columns for support and had no walls on any side. If this construction method was similar to the original, then Leshan Hall would not have been suitable for monastic practice; furthermore, its location at the village entrance, where followers tended to gather, rather than in a secluded area suitable for monks, indicates that Leshan Hall did not possess the typical characteristics of a {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=Temple}}, but rather those of a dining hall. This is one possible reason for the renaming from Longshou Temple to Leshan Hall.
<ref name=":0" />

== Related Activities ==
Every year on the {{ill|Ghost Festival|de|Geisterfest|ja|中元節|zh|中元节|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ghost Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ulambana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}} (the fifteenth day of the seventh month of the {{ill|Lunar Calendar|de|Mondkalender|ja|農暦|zh|农历|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国暦|1=Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134032}}), the Manichaean believers of Shangwan Village gather at Leshan Hall to perform a ritual for the transference of the souls of the deceased. This concept of soul transference is at odds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light, indicating that Manichaeism in Xiapu, in order to become popular and endure locally, had incorporated some unique elements—such as this soul-transference ritual—that provided comfort to the people.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=马小鹤 |author2=吴春明 |title=摩尼教与济度亡灵——霞浦明教《奏申牒书科册》研究 |journal=九州学林 |language=zh |publication-date=2010 |issue=03 |page=45-46}}</ref>

  A collection of important documents on Manichaeism in Xiapu, it is currently the longest extant collection of Manichaean Chinese texts, with an account of Leshan Hall found in the manuscript "Leshan Hall Divine Record".
* {{ill|Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|de|Manichäische Steinskulpturen aus Shangwan Village|ja|上万村摩尼教石彫造像|zh|上万村摩尼教石雕造像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Built near Leshan Hall to adorn the Three Buddha Pagoda behind the tower and the Dengdong Stone Pagoda, it is one of the few surviving examples of Manichaean stone carving art.

  Located outside the south gate of {{ill|Fuzhou|en|Fuzhou|WD=Q68481|lt=Fuzhou City}}, it is the best-preserved Manichaean palace building, enshrining Lin Deng who once aided in disaster relief in Fuzhou.

'''Leshan Hall''', commonly known as '''Gaizhu Hall''', formerly known as '''Longshou Temple''', is located in China, {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian Province}}, {{ill|Ningde|en|Ningde|WD=Q68919|lt=Ningde City}}, {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County}}, {{ill|Baiyang Township|de|Baiyang Township|ja|柏洋郷|zh|柏洋乡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baiyang Township|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|de|Tahu-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|塔後村 (霞浦県)|zh|塔后村|lt=Tahu Village|lt_ja=塔後村|lt_de=Tahu-Dorf|lt_zh=塔后村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|de|Shangwan-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|上万村 (霞浦県)|zh|上万村|lt=Shangwan Natural Village|lt_ja=上万自然村|lt_de=Shangwan-Naturdorf|lt_zh=上万自然村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was established by Fujian {{ill|Mingjiao|de|Mingjiao|ja|明教|zh|明教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mingjiao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐代三夷教|1=Three Persian religions in Tang dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418516}} ({{ill|Manichaeism|en|Manichaeism|WD=Q131165|lt=Manichaeism}}) follower {{ill|Sun Mian|de|Sun Mian|ja|孫綿|zh|孙绵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Mian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} during the {{ill|Northern Song|de|Nördliche Song-Dynastie|ja|北宋|zh|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} period in the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD), making it the earliest Manichaean building constructed in Fujian. For over a thousand years, it served as an important venue for Manichaean activities in the Baiyang area until it was destroyed in 2006 by {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Sangmei Typhoon|lt_ja=桑美台風|lt_de=Sangmei-Taifun|lt_zh=桑美台风|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |author=樊丽沙 |author2=杨富学 |title=霞浦摩尼教文献及其重要性 |journal=世界宗教研究 |language=zh |publisher=中国社会科学院世界宗教研究所 |publication-place=北京 |publication-date=2011 |issue=06 |issn=1000-4289}}</ref>
<ref name="Kósa2018">{{Cite book|last=Kósa|first=Gábor|title=Der östliche Manichäismus im Spiegel seiner Buch- und Schriftkultur|publisher=Walter de Gruyter GmbH & Co KG|date=2018|isbn=978-3-11-059393-8|language=de}}</ref>

== Name ==
Leshan Hall was originally established as "Longshou Temple" and was renamed "Leshan Hall" during the Yuan dynasty. Initially, the name "Leshan" was believed to be derived from the phrase "The wise delight in water, and the benevolent delight in mountains" found in {{ill|Analects|en|Analects|WD=Q276015|lt=The Analects}} (Lunyu: Yong Ye). Another theory suggests that the term "Leshan" originates from {{ill|Middle Persian|de|Mittelpersisch|ja|中古ペルシア語|zh|中古波斯语|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Persian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{Lang|pal|Rōšan}} (or {{Lang|pal|Rōšn}}), meaning "light", which corresponds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light. Moreover, since the building was more likely used as a dining hall for followers rather than as a monastery (see the "Architectural Style and Nature" section), the change from "Temple" (寺) to "Hall" (堂) was probably due to this reason.
<ref name=":0" />

== Historical Development ==
According to the explicit records in the "Sun Family Genealogy of the Republic of China" compiled in the 21st year of the Republic (1932), Sun Mian from Chanyang Village (now also known as Shenyang Village, under the jurisdiction of Baiyang Township) purchased the land during the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD) and commenced the construction of Longshou Temple, becoming its first abbot. In the fifth year of {{ill|Tiansheng|de|Tiansheng|ja|天聖|zh|天圣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tiansheng|1=Tiansheng|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939230}} (1027 AD), a resident of Shangwan Village, {{ill|Lin Deng|de|Lin Deng|ja|林瞪|zh|林瞪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lin Deng|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, converted to Mingjiao at Longshou Temple, later becoming the leading missionary master of Mingjiao in the area and the second abbot of Longshou Temple. His direct disciple, Chen Pingshan, succeeded him.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=杨富学 |title=林瞪及其在中国摩尼教史上的地位 |journal=中国史研究 |language=zh |publication-place=北京市 |publication-date=2014 |issue=01 |page=109-124 |issn=1002-7963}}</ref>

During the Yuan dynasty, Longshou Temple was renamed Leshan Hall, and during the Ming and Qing dynasties, Leshan Hall underwent two major renovations. In the early years of the People's Republic of China, according to local elders, Leshan Hall still had several courtyards. At the beginning of the {{ill|Cultural Revolution|en|Cultural Revolution|WD=Q8690|lt=Cultural Revolution}}, Leshan Hall was demolished and subsequently received only simple repairs with makeshift shed-like structures used for storing villagers' farming tools and for temporary shelter from rain. In 2006, Leshan Hall was destroyed by the {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Super Typhoon Sangmei|lt_ja=超強台風桑美|lt_de=Super-Taifun Sangmei|lt_zh=超强台风桑美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leaving only the foundation columns, steps, and the base of the shrine.
<ref name=":0" />

== Architectural Style and Nature ==
Due to its great antiquity and the lack of written records, the original appearance of Leshan Hall is unknown. The site covers an area of approximately 1560 square meters. Based on photographs of the makeshift structure built at the end of the Cultural Revolution, Leshan Hall relied on columns for support and had no walls on any side. If this construction method was similar to the original, then Leshan Hall would not have been suitable for monastic practice; furthermore, its location at the village entrance, where followers tended to gather, rather than in a secluded area suitable for monks, indicates that Leshan Hall did not possess the typical characteristics of a {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=Temple}}, but rather those of a dining hall. This is one possible reason for the renaming from Longshou Temple to Leshan Hall.
<ref name=":0" />

== Related Activities ==
Every year on the {{ill|Ghost Festival|de|Geisterfest|ja|中元節|zh|中元节|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ghost Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ulambana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}} (the fifteenth day of the seventh month of the {{ill|Lunar Calendar|de|Mondkalender|ja|農暦|zh|农历|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国暦|1=Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134032}}), the Manichaean believers of Shangwan Village gather at Leshan Hall to perform a ritual for the transference of the souls of the deceased. This concept of soul transference is at odds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light, indicating that Manichaeism in Xiapu, in order to become popular and endure locally, had incorporated some unique elements—such as this soul-transference ritual—that provided comfort to the people.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=马小鹤 |author2=吴春明 |title=摩尼教与济度亡灵——霞浦明教《奏申牒书科册》研究 |journal=九州学林 |language=zh |publication-date=2010 |issue=03 |page=45-46}}</ref>


== See Also ==
* {{ill|Xiapu Documents|de|Xiapu-Dokumente|ja|霞浦文書|zh|霞浦文书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiapu Documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  A collection of important documents on Manichaeism in Xiapu, it is currently the longest extant collection of Manichaean Chinese texts, with an account of Leshan Hall found in the manuscript "Leshan Hall Divine Record".
* {{ill|Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|de|Manichäische Steinskulpturen aus Shangwan Village|ja|上万村摩尼教石彫造像|zh|上万村摩尼教石雕造像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Built near Leshan Hall to adorn the Three Buddha Pagoda behind the tower and the Dengdong Stone Pagoda, it is one of the few surviving examples of Manichaean stone carving art.
* {{ill|Fushou Palace in Puxi|de|Fushou-Palast in Puxi|ja|浦西境福寿宮|zh|浦西境福寿宫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushou Palace in Puxi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Located outside the south gate of {{ill|Fuzhou|en|Fuzhou|WD=Q68481|lt=Fuzhou City}}, it is the best-preserved Manichaean palace building, enshrining Lin Deng who once aided in disaster relief in Fuzhou.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[zh:乐山堂]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[zh:乐山堂]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[zh:乐山堂]]



{{Not|Longshou Temple}}
{{Coord|27|05|58.6|N|119|54|49.4|E|display=title}}
[[File:泗洲文佛像和乐山堂遗址.jpg|thumb|Photo of the Leshan Hall ruins exhibited at the {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County}} Museum]]

'''Leshan Hall''', commonly known as '''Gaizhu Hall''', formerly known as '''Longshou Temple''', is located in China, {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian Province}}, {{ill|Ningde|en|Ningde|WD=Q68919|lt=Ningde City}}, {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County}}, {{ill|Baiyang Township|de|Baiyang Township|ja|柏洋郷|zh|柏洋乡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baiyang Township|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|de|Tahu-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|塔後村 (霞浦県)|zh|塔后村|lt=Tahu Village|lt_ja=塔後村|lt_de=Tahu-Dorf|lt_zh=塔后村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|de|Shangwan-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|上万村 (霞浦県)|zh|上万村|lt=Shangwan Natural Village|lt_ja=上万自然村|lt_de=Shangwan-Naturdorf|lt_zh=上万自然村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was established by Fujian {{ill|Mingjiao|de|Mingjiao|ja|明教|zh|明教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mingjiao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐代三夷教|1=Three Persian religions in Tang dynasty|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418516}} ({{ill|Manichaeism|en|Manichaeism|WD=Q131165|lt=Manichaeism}}) follower {{ill|Sun Mian|de|Sun Mian|ja|孫綿|zh|孙绵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Mian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} during the {{ill|Northern Song|de|Nördliche Song-Dynastie|ja|北宋|zh|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song dynasty|WD=Q319460}} period in the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD), making it the earliest Manichaean building constructed in Fujian. For over a thousand years, it served as an important venue for Manichaean activities in the Baiyang area until it was destroyed in 2006 by {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Sangmei Typhoon|lt_ja=桑美台風|lt_de=Sangmei-Taifun|lt_zh=桑美台风|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |author=樊丽沙 |author2=杨富学 |title=霞浦摩尼教文献及其重要性 |journal=世界宗教研究 |language=zh |publisher=中国社会科学院世界宗教研究所 |publication-place=北京 |publication-date=2011 |issue=06 |issn=1000-4289}}</ref>
<ref name="Kósa2018">{{Cite book|last=Kósa|first=Gábor|title=Der östliche Manichäismus im Spiegel seiner Buch- und Schriftkultur|publisher=Walter de Gruyter GmbH & Co KG|date=2018|isbn=978-3-11-059393-8|language=de}}</ref>

== Name ==
Leshan Hall was originally established as "Longshou Temple" and was renamed "Leshan Hall" during the Yuan dynasty. Initially, the name "Leshan" was believed to be derived from the phrase "The wise delight in water, and the benevolent delight in mountains" found in {{ill|Analects|en|Analects|WD=Q276015|lt=The Analects}} (Lunyu: Yong Ye). Another theory suggests that the term "Leshan" originates from {{ill|Middle Persian|de|Mittelpersisch|ja|中古ペルシア語|zh|中古波斯语|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Persian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{Lang|pal|Rōšan}} (or {{Lang|pal|Rōšn}}), meaning "light", which corresponds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light. Moreover, since the building was more likely used as a dining hall for followers rather than as a monastery (see the "Architectural Style and Nature" section), the change from "Temple" (寺) to "Hall" (堂) was probably due to this reason.
<ref name=":0" />

== Historical Development ==
According to the explicit records in the "Sun Family Genealogy of the Republic of China" compiled in the 21st year of the Republic (1932), Sun Mian from Chanyang Village (now also known as Shenyang Village, under the jurisdiction of Baiyang Township) purchased the land during the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD) and commenced the construction of Longshou Temple, becoming its first abbot. In the fifth year of {{ill|Tiansheng|de|Tiansheng|ja|天聖|zh|天圣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tiansheng|1=Tiansheng|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939230}} (1027 AD), a resident of Shangwan Village, {{ill|Lin Deng|de|Lin Deng|ja|林瞪|zh|林瞪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lin Deng|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, converted to Mingjiao at Longshou Temple, later becoming the leading missionary master of Mingjiao in the area and the second abbot of Longshou Temple. His direct disciple, Chen Pingshan, succeeded him.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=杨富学 |title=林瞪及其在中国摩尼教史上的地位 |journal=中国史研究 |language=zh |publication-place=北京市 |publication-date=2014 |issue=01 |page=109-124 |issn=1002-7963}}</ref>

During the Yuan dynasty, Longshou Temple was renamed Leshan Hall, and during the Ming and Qing dynasties, Leshan Hall underwent two major renovations. In the early years of the People's Republic of China, according to local elders, Leshan Hall still had several courtyards. At the beginning of the {{ill|Cultural Revolution|en|Cultural Revolution|WD=Q8690|lt=Cultural Revolution}}, Leshan Hall was demolished and subsequently received only simple repairs with makeshift shed-like structures used for storing villagers' farming tools and for temporary shelter from rain. In 2006, Leshan Hall was destroyed by the {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Super Typhoon Sangmei|lt_ja=超強台風桑美|lt_de=Super-Taifun Sangmei|lt_zh=超强台风桑美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leaving only the foundation columns, steps, and the base of the shrine.
<ref name=":0" />

== Architectural Style and Nature ==
Due to its great antiquity and the lack of written records, the original appearance of Leshan Hall is unknown. The site covers an area of approximately 1560 square meters. Based on photographs of the makeshift structure built at the end of the Cultural Revolution, Leshan Hall relied on columns for support and had no walls on any side. If this construction method was similar to the original, then Leshan Hall would not have been suitable for monastic practice; furthermore, its location at the village entrance, where followers tended to gather, rather than in a secluded area suitable for monks, indicates that Leshan Hall did not possess the typical characteristics of a {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=Temple}}, but rather those of a dining hall. This is one possible reason for the renaming from Longshou Temple to Leshan Hall.
<ref name=":0" />

== Related Activities ==
Every year on the {{ill|Ghost Festival|de|Geisterfest|ja|中元節|zh|中元节|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ghost Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ulambana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}} (the fifteenth day of the seventh month of the {{ill|Lunar Calendar|de|Mondkalender|ja|農暦|zh|农历|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国暦|1=Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134032}}), the Manichaean believers of Shangwan Village gather at Leshan Hall to perform a ritual for the transference of the souls of the deceased. This concept of soul transference is at odds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light, indicating that Manichaeism in Xiapu, in order to become popular and endure locally, had incorporated some unique elements—such as this soul-transference ritual—that provided comfort to the people.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=马小鹤 |author2=吴春明 |title=摩尼教与济度亡灵——霞浦明教《奏申牒书科册》研究 |journal=九州学林 |language=zh |publication-date=2010 |issue=03 |page=45-46}}</ref>


== See Also ==
* {{ill|Xiapu Documents|de|Xiapu-Dokumente|ja|霞浦文書|zh|霞浦文书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiapu Documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  A collection of important documents on Manichaeism in Xiapu, it is currently the longest extant collection of Manichaean Chinese texts, with an account of Leshan Hall found in the manuscript "Leshan Hall Divine Record".
* {{ill|Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|de|Manichäische Steinskulpturen aus Shangwan Village|ja|上万村摩尼教石彫造像|zh|上万村摩尼教石雕造像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Built near Leshan Hall to adorn the Three Buddha Pagoda behind the tower and the Dengdong Stone Pagoda, it is one of the few surviving examples of Manichaean stone carving art.
* {{ill|Fushou Palace in Puxi|de|Fushou-Palast in Puxi|ja|浦西境福寿宮|zh|浦西境福寿宫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushou Palace in Puxi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Located outside the south gate of {{ill|Fuzhou|en|Fuzhou|WD=Q68481|lt=Fuzhou City}}, it is the best-preserved Manichaean palace building, enshrining Lin Deng who once aided in disaster relief in Fuzhou.

== References ==

{{ill|Established in 966|de|Gegründet 966|ja|966年建立|zh|966年建立|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Established in 966|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|Defunct Buildings in Fujian|de|Gebäude in Fujian, die nicht mehr existieren|ja|福建に存在しない建築物|zh|福建已不存在的建筑物|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Defunct Buildings in Fujian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}
{{ill|History of Xiapu County|de|Geschichte des Xiapu County|ja|霞浦県の歴史|zh|霞浦县历史|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Xiapu County|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}

== See Also ==
* {{ill|Xiapu Documents|de|Xiapu-Dokumente|ja|霞浦文書|zh|霞浦文书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiapu Documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  A collection of important documents on Manichaeism in Xiapu, it is currently the longest extant collection of Manichaean Chinese texts, with an account of Leshan Hall found in the manuscript "Leshan Hall Divine Record".
* {{ill|Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|de|Manichäische Steinskulpturen aus Shangwan Village|ja|上万村摩尼教石彫造像|zh|上万村摩尼教石雕造像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Built near Leshan Hall to adorn the Three Buddha Pagoda behind the tower and the Dengdong Stone Pagoda, it is one of the few surviving examples of Manichaean stone carving art.
* {{ill|Fushou Palace in Puxi|de|Fushou-Palast in Puxi|ja|浦西境福寿宮|zh|浦西境福寿宫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushou Palace in Puxi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Located outside the south gate of {{ill|Fuzhou|en|Fuzhou|WD=Q68481|lt=Fuzhou City|1=Fuzhou}}, it is the best-preserved Manichaean palace building, enshrining Lin Deng who once aided in disaster relief in Fuzhou.

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[zh:乐山堂]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[zh:乐山堂]]
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[zh:乐山堂]]



{{Not|Longshou Temple}}
{{Coord|27|05|58.6|N|119|54|49.4|E|display=title}}
[[File:泗洲文佛像和乐山堂遗址.jpg|thumb|Photo of the Leshan Hall ruins exhibited at the {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County|1=Xiapu}} Museum]]

'''Leshan Hall''', commonly known as '''Gaizhu Hall''', formerly known as '''Longshou Temple''', is located in China, {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian Province|1=Fujian}}, {{ill|Ningde|en|Ningde|WD=Q68919|lt=Ningde City|1=Ningde}}, {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County|1=Xiapu}}, {{ill|Baiyang Township|de|Baiyang Township|ja|柏洋郷|zh|柏洋乡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baiyang Township|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|de|Tahu-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|塔後村 (霞浦県)|zh|塔后村|lt=Tahu Village|lt_ja=塔後村|lt_de=Tahu-Dorf|lt_zh=塔后村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|de|Shangwan-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|上万村 (霞浦県)|zh|上万村|lt=Shangwan Natural Village|lt_ja=上万自然村|lt_de=Shangwan-Naturdorf|lt_zh=上万自然村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was established by Fujian {{ill|Mingjiao|de|Mingjiao|ja|明教|zh|明教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mingjiao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐代三夷教|1=Three Persian religions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418516}} ({{ill|Manichaeism|en|Manichaeism|WD=Q131165|lt=Manichaeism|1=Manichaeism}}) follower {{ill|Sun Mian|de|Sun Mian|ja|孫綿|zh|孙绵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Mian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} during the {{ill|Northern Song|de|Nördliche Song-Dynastie|ja|北宋|zh|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song (960–1127)|WD=Q319460}} period in the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD), making it the earliest Manichaean building constructed in Fujian. For over a thousand years, it served as an important venue for Manichaean activities in the Baiyang area until it was destroyed in 2006 by {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Sangmei Typhoon|lt_ja=桑美台風|lt_de=Sangmei-Taifun|lt_zh=桑美台风|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |author=樊丽沙 |author2=杨富学 |title=霞浦摩尼教文献及其重要性 |journal=世界宗教研究 |language=zh |publisher=中国社会科学院世界宗教研究所 |publication-place=北京 |publication-date=2011 |issue=06 |issn=1000-4289}}</ref>
<ref name="Kósa2018">{{Cite book|last=Kósa|first=Gábor|title=Der östliche Manichäismus im Spiegel seiner Buch- und Schriftkultur|publisher=Walter de Gruyter GmbH & Co KG|date=2018|isbn=978-3-11-059393-8|language=de}}</ref>

== Name ==
Leshan Hall was originally established as "Longshou Temple" and was renamed "Leshan Hall" during the Yuan dynasty. Initially, the name "Leshan" was believed to be derived from the phrase "The wise delight in water, and the benevolent delight in mountains" found in {{ill|Analects|en|Analects|WD=Q276015|lt=The Analects|1=Analects}} (Lunyu: Yong Ye). Another theory suggests that the term "Leshan" originates from {{ill|Middle Persian|de|Mittelpersisch|ja|中古ペルシア語|zh|中古波斯语|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Persian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{Lang|pal|Rōšan}} (or {{Lang|pal|Rōšn}}), meaning "light", which corresponds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light. Moreover, since the building was more likely used as a dining hall for followers rather than as a monastery (see the "Architectural Style and Nature" section), the change from "Temple" (寺) to "Hall" (堂) was probably due to this reason.
<ref name=":0" />

== Historical Development ==
According to the explicit records in the "Sun Family Genealogy of the Republic of China" compiled in the 21st year of the Republic (1932), Sun Mian from Chanyang Village (now also known as Shenyang Village, under the jurisdiction of Baiyang Township) purchased the land during the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD) and commenced the construction of Longshou Temple, becoming its first abbot. In the fifth year of {{ill|Tiansheng|de|Tiansheng|ja|天聖|zh|天圣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tiansheng|1=Tiansheng|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939230}} (1027 AD), a resident of Shangwan Village, {{ill|Lin Deng|de|Lin Deng|ja|林瞪|zh|林瞪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lin Deng|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, converted to Mingjiao at Longshou Temple, later becoming the leading missionary master of Mingjiao in the area and the second abbot of Longshou Temple. His direct disciple, Chen Pingshan, succeeded him.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=杨富学 |title=林瞪及其在中国摩尼教史上的地位 |journal=中国史研究 |language=zh |publication-place=北京市 |publication-date=2014 |issue=01 |page=109-124 |issn=1002-7963}}</ref>

During the Yuan dynasty, Longshou Temple was renamed Leshan Hall, and during the Ming and Qing dynasties, Leshan Hall underwent two major renovations. In the early years of the People's Republic of China, according to local elders, Leshan Hall still had several courtyards. At the beginning of the {{ill|Cultural Revolution|en|Cultural Revolution|WD=Q8690|lt=Cultural Revolution|1=Cultural Revolution}}, Leshan Hall was demolished and subsequently received only simple repairs with makeshift shed-like structures used for storing villagers' farming tools and for temporary shelter from rain. In 2006, Leshan Hall was destroyed by the {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Super Typhoon Sangmei|lt_ja=超強台風桑美|lt_de=Super-Taifun Sangmei|lt_zh=超强台风桑美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leaving only the foundation columns, steps, and the base of the shrine.
<ref name=":0" />

== Architectural Style and Nature ==
Due to its great antiquity and the lack of written records, the original appearance of Leshan Hall is unknown. The site covers an area of approximately 1560 square meters. Based on photographs of the makeshift structure built at the end of the Cultural Revolution, Leshan Hall relied on columns for support and had no walls on any side. If this construction method was similar to the original, then Leshan Hall would not have been suitable for monastic practice; furthermore, its location at the village entrance, where followers tended to gather, rather than in a secluded area suitable for monks, indicates that Leshan Hall did not possess the typical characteristics of a {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=Temple|1=Buddhist temple}}, but rather those of a dining hall. This is one possible reason for the renaming from Longshou Temple to Leshan Hall.
<ref name=":0" />

== Related Activities ==
Every year on the {{ill|Ghost Festival|de|Geisterfest|ja|中元節|zh|中元节|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ghost Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ghost Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}} (the fifteenth day of the seventh month of the {{ill|Lunar Calendar|de|Mondkalender|ja|農暦|zh|农历|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国暦|1=Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134032}}), the Manichaean believers of Shangwan Village gather at Leshan Hall to perform a ritual for the transference of the souls of the deceased. This concept of soul transference is at odds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light, indicating that Manichaeism in Xiapu, in order to become popular and endure locally, had incorporated some unique elements—such as this soul-transference ritual—that provided comfort to the people.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=马小鹤 |author2=吴春明 |title=摩尼教与济度亡灵——霞浦明教《奏申牒书科册》研究 |journal=九州学林 |language=zh |publication-date=2010 |issue=03 |page=45-46}}</ref>

  A collection of important documents on Manichaeism in Xiapu, it is currently the longest extant collection of Manichaean Chinese texts, with an account of Leshan Hall found in the manuscript "Leshan Hall Divine Record".

  Built near Leshan Hall to adorn the Three Buddha Pagoda behind the tower and the Dengdong Stone Pagoda, it is one of the few surviving examples of Manichaean stone carving art.

  Located outside the south gate of {{ill|Fuzhou|en|Fuzhou|WD=Q68481|lt=Fuzhou City|1=Fuzhou}}, it is the best-preserved Manichaean palace building, enshrining Lin Deng who once aided in disaster relief in Fuzhou.

In the early years of the People's Republic of China, according to local elders, Leshan Hall still had several courtyards. At the beginning of the {{ill|Cultural Revolution|en|Cultural Revolution|WD=Q8690|lt=Cultural Revolution|1=Cultural Revolution}}, Leshan Hall was demolished and subsequently received only simple repairs with makeshift shed-like structures used for storing villagers' farming tools and for temporary shelter from rain. In 2006, Leshan Hall was destroyed by the {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Super Typhoon Sangmei|lt_ja=超強台風桑美|lt_de=Super-Taifun Sangmei|lt_zh=超强台风桑美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leaving only the foundation columns, steps, and the base of the shrine.
<ref name=":0" />

== Architectural Style and Nature ==
Due to its great antiquity and the lack of written records, the original appearance of Leshan Hall is unknown. The site covers an area of approximately 1560 square meters. Based on photographs of the makeshift structure built at the end of the Cultural Revolution, Leshan Hall relied on columns for support and had no walls on any side. If this construction method was similar to the original, then Leshan Hall would not have been suitable for monastic practice; furthermore, its location at the village entrance, where followers tended to gather, rather than in a secluded area suitable for monks, indicates that Leshan Hall did not possess the typical characteristics of a {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=Temple|1=Buddhist temple}}, but rather those of a dining hall. This is one possible reason for the renaming from Longshou Temple to Leshan Hall.
<ref name=":0" />

== Related Activities ==
Every year on the {{ill|Ghost Festival|de|Geisterfest|ja|中元節|zh|中元节|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ghost Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ghost Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}} (the fifteenth day of the seventh month of the {{ill|Lunar Calendar|de|Mondkalender|ja|農暦|zh|农历|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国暦|1=Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134032}}), the Manichaean believers of Shangwan Village gather at Leshan Hall to perform a ritual for the transference of the souls of the deceased. This concept of soul transference is at odds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light, indicating that Manichaeism in Xiapu, in order to become popular and endure locally, had incorporated some unique elements—such as this soul-transference ritual—that provided comfort to the people.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=马小鹤 |author2=吴春明 |title=摩尼教与济度亡灵——霞浦明教《奏申牒书科册》研究 |journal=九州学林 |language=zh |publication-date=2010 |issue=03 |page=45-46}}</ref>


== See Also ==
* {{ill|Xiapu Documents|de|Xiapu-Dokumente|ja|霞浦文書|zh|霞浦文书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiapu Documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  A collection of important documents on Manichaeism in Xiapu, it is currently the longest extant collection of Manichaean Chinese texts, with an account of Leshan Hall found in the manuscript "Leshan Hall Divine Record".
* {{ill|Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|de|Manichäische Steinskulpturen aus Shangwan Village|ja|上万村摩尼教石彫造像|zh|上万村摩尼教石雕造像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Built near Leshan Hall to adorn the Three Buddha Pagoda behind the tower and the Dengdong Stone Pagoda, it is one of the few surviving examples of Manichaean stone carving art.
* {{ill|Fushou Palace in Puxi|de|Fushou-Palast in Puxi|ja|浦西境福寿宮|zh|浦西境福寿宫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushou Palace in Puxi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Located outside the south gate of {{ill|Fuzhou|en|Fuzhou|WD=Q68481|lt=Fuzhou City|1=Fuzhou}}, it is the best-preserved Manichaean palace building, enshrining Lin Deng who once aided in disaster relief in Fuzhou.

{{Not|Longshou Temple}}
{{Coord|27|05|58.6|N|119|54|49.4|E|display=title}}
[[File:泗洲文佛像和乐山堂遗址.jpg|thumb|Photo of the Leshan Hall ruins exhibited at the {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County|1=Xiapu}} Museum]]

'''Leshan Hall''', commonly known as '''Gaizhu Hall''', formerly known as '''Longshou Temple''', is located in China, {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian Province|1=Fujian}}, {{ill|Ningde|en|Ningde|WD=Q68919|lt=Ningde City|1=Ningde}}, {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County|1=Xiapu}}, {{ill|Baiyang Township|de|Baiyang Township|ja|柏洋郷|zh|柏洋乡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baiyang Township|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|de|Tahu-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|塔後村 (霞浦県)|zh|塔后村|lt=Tahu Village|lt_ja=塔後村|lt_de=Tahu-Dorf|lt_zh=塔后村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|de|Shangwan-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|上万村 (霞浦県)|zh|上万村|lt=Shangwan Natural Village|lt_ja=上万自然村|lt_de=Shangwan-Naturdorf|lt_zh=上万自然村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was established by Fujian {{ill|Mingjiao|de|Mingjiao|ja|明教|zh|明教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mingjiao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐代三夷教|1=Three Persian religions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418516}} ({{ill|Manichaeism|en|Manichaeism|WD=Q131165|lt=Manichaeism|1=Manichaeism}}) follower {{ill|Sun Mian|de|Sun Mian|ja|孫綿|zh|孙绵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Mian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} during the {{ill|Northern Song|de|Nördliche Song-Dynastie|ja|北宋|zh|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song (960–1127)|WD=Q319460}} period in the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD), making it the earliest Manichaean building constructed in Fujian. For over a thousand years, it served as an important venue for Manichaean activities in the Baiyang area until it was destroyed in 2006 by {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Sangmei Typhoon|lt_ja=桑美台風|lt_de=Sangmei-Taifun|lt_zh=桑美台风|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |author=樊丽沙 |author2=杨富学 |title=霞浦摩尼教文献及其重要性 |journal=世界宗教研究 |language=zh |publisher=中国社会科学院世界宗教研究所 |publication-place=北京 |publication-date=2011 |issue=06 |issn=1000-4289}}</ref>
<ref name="Kósa2018">{{Cite book|last=Kósa|first=Gábor|title=Der östliche Manichäismus im Spiegel seiner Buch- und Schriftkultur|publisher=Walter de Gruyter GmbH & Co KG|date=2018|isbn=978-3-11-059393-8|language=de}}</ref>

== Name ==
Leshan Hall was originally established as "Longshou Temple" and was renamed "Leshan Hall" during the Yuan dynasty. Initially, the name "Leshan" was believed to be derived from the phrase "The wise delight in water, and the benevolent delight in mountains" found in {{ill|Analects|en|Analects|WD=Q276015|lt=The Analects|1=Analects}} (Lunyu: Yong Ye). Another theory suggests that the term "Leshan" originates from {{ill|Middle Persian|de|Mittelpersisch|ja|中古ペルシア語|zh|中古波斯语|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Persian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{Lang|pal|Rōšan}} (or {{Lang|pal|Rōšn}}), meaning "light", which corresponds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light. Moreover, since the building was more likely used as a dining hall for followers rather than as a monastery (see the "Architectural Style and Nature" section), the change from "Temple" (寺) to "Hall" (堂) was probably due to this reason.
<ref name=":0" />

== Historical Development ==
According to the explicit records in the "Sun Family Genealogy of the Republic of China" compiled in the 21st year of the Republic (1932), Sun Mian from Chanyang Village (now also known as Shenyang Village, under the jurisdiction of Baiyang Township) purchased the land during the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD) and commenced the construction of Longshou Temple, becoming its first abbot. In the fifth year of {{ill|Tiansheng|de|Tiansheng|ja|天聖|zh|天圣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tiansheng|1=Tiansheng|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939230}} (1027 AD), a resident of Shangwan Village, {{ill|Lin Deng|de|Lin Deng|ja|林瞪|zh|林瞪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lin Deng|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, converted to Mingjiao at Longshou Temple, later becoming the leading missionary master of Mingjiao in the area and the second abbot of Longshou Temple. His direct disciple, Chen Pingshan, succeeded him.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=杨富学 |title=林瞪及其在中国摩尼教史上的地位 |journal=中国史研究 |language=zh |publication-place=北京市 |publication-date=2014 |issue=01 |page=109-124 |issn=1002-7963}}</ref>

During the Yuan dynasty, Longshou Temple was renamed Leshan Hall, and during the Ming and Qing dynasties, Leshan Hall underwent two major renovations. In the early years of the People's Republic of China, according to local elders, Leshan Hall still had several courtyards. At the beginning of the {{ill|Cultural Revolution|en|Cultural Revolution|WD=Q8690|lt=Cultural Revolution|1=Cultural Revolution}}, Leshan Hall was demolished and subsequently received only simple repairs with makeshift shed-like structures used for storing villagers' farming tools and for temporary shelter from rain. In 2006, Leshan Hall was destroyed by the {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Super Typhoon Sangmei|lt_ja=超強台風桑美|lt_de=Super-Taifun Sangmei|lt_zh=超强台风桑美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leaving only the foundation columns, steps, and the base of the shrine.
<ref name=":0" />

== Architectural Style and Nature ==
Due to its great antiquity and the lack of written records, the original appearance of Leshan Hall is unknown. The site covers an area of approximately 1560 square meters. Based on photographs of the makeshift structure built at the end of the Cultural Revolution, Leshan Hall relied on columns for support and had no walls on any side. If this construction method was similar to the original, then Leshan Hall would not have been suitable for monastic practice; furthermore, its location at the village entrance, where followers tended to gather, rather than in a secluded area suitable for monks, indicates that Leshan Hall did not possess the typical characteristics of a {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=Temple|1=Buddhist temple}}, but rather those of a dining hall. This is one possible reason for the renaming from Longshou Temple to Leshan Hall.
<ref name=":0" />

== Related Activities ==
Every year on the {{ill|Ghost Festival|de|Geisterfest|ja|中元節|zh|中元节|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ghost Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ghost Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}} (the fifteenth day of the seventh month of the {{ill|Lunar Calendar|de|Mondkalender|ja|農暦|zh|农历|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国暦|1=Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134032}}), the Manichaean believers of Shangwan Village gather at Leshan Hall to perform a ritual for the transference of the souls of the deceased. This concept of soul transference is at odds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light, indicating that Manichaeism in Xiapu, in order to become popular and endure locally, had incorporated some unique elements—such as this soul-transference ritual—that provided comfort to the people.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=马小鹤 |author2=吴春明 |title=摩尼教与济度亡灵——霞浦明教《奏申牒书科册》研究 |journal=九州学林 |language=zh |publication-date=2010 |issue=03 |page=45-46}}</ref>

  A collection of important documents on Manichaeism in Xiapu, it is currently the longest extant collection of Manichaean Chinese texts, with an account of Leshan Hall found in the manuscript "Leshan Hall Divine Record".
* {{ill|Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|de|Manichäische Steinskulpturen aus Shangwan Village|ja|上万村摩尼教石彫造像|zh|上万村摩尼教石雕造像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Built near Leshan Hall to adorn the Three Buddha Pagoda behind the tower and the Dengdong Stone Pagoda, it is one of the few surviving examples of Manichaean stone carving art.

  Located outside the south gate of {{ill|Fuzhou|en|Fuzhou|WD=Q68481|lt=Fuzhou City|1=Fuzhou}}, it is the best-preserved Manichaean palace building, enshrining Lin Deng who once aided in disaster relief in Fuzhou.

Leshan Hall was originally established as "Longshou Temple" and was renamed "Leshan Hall" during the Yuan dynasty. Initially, the name "Leshan" was believed to be derived from the phrase "The wise delight in water, and the benevolent delight in mountains" found in {{ill|Analects|en|Analects|WD=Q276015|lt=The Analects|1=Analects}} (Lunyu: Yong Ye). Another theory suggests that the term "Leshan" originates from {{ill|Middle Persian|de|Mittelpersisch|ja|中古ペルシア語|zh|中古波斯语|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Persian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{Lang|pal|Rōšan}} (or {{Lang|pal|Rōšn}}), meaning "light", which corresponds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light. Moreover, since the building was more likely used as a dining hall for followers rather than as a monastery (see the "Architectural Style and Nature" section), the change from "Temple" (寺) to "Hall" (堂) was probably due to this reason.
<ref name=":0" />

== Historical Development ==
According to the explicit records in the "Sun Family Genealogy of the Republic of China" compiled in the 21st year of the Republic (1932), Sun Mian from Chanyang Village (now also known as Shenyang Village, under the jurisdiction of Baiyang Township) purchased the land during the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD) and commenced the construction of Longshou Temple, becoming its first abbot. In the fifth year of {{ill|Tiansheng|de|Tiansheng|ja|天聖|zh|天圣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tiansheng|1=Tiansheng|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939230}} (1027 AD), a resident of Shangwan Village, {{ill|Lin Deng|de|Lin Deng|ja|林瞪|zh|林瞪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lin Deng|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, converted to Mingjiao at Longshou Temple, later becoming the leading missionary master of Mingjiao in the area and the second abbot of Longshou Temple. His direct disciple, Chen Pingshan, succeeded him.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=杨富学 |title=林瞪及其在中国摩尼教史上的地位 |journal=中国史研究 |language=zh |publication-place=北京市 |publication-date=2014 |issue=01 |page=109-124 |issn=1002-7963}}</ref>

During the Yuan dynasty, Longshou Temple was renamed Leshan Hall, and during the Ming and Qing dynasties, Leshan Hall underwent two major renovations. In the early years of the People's Republic of China, according to local elders, Leshan Hall still had several courtyards. At the beginning of the {{ill|Cultural Revolution|en|Cultural Revolution|WD=Q8690|lt=Cultural Revolution|1=Cultural Revolution}}, Leshan Hall was demolished and subsequently received only simple repairs with makeshift shed-like structures used for storing villagers' farming tools and for temporary shelter from rain. In 2006, Leshan Hall was destroyed by the {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Super Typhoon Sangmei|lt_ja=超強台風桑美|lt_de=Super-Taifun Sangmei|lt_zh=超强台风桑美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leaving only the foundation columns, steps, and the base of the shrine.
<ref name=":0" />

== Architectural Style and Nature ==
Due to its great antiquity and the lack of written records, the original appearance of Leshan Hall is unknown. The site covers an area of approximately 1560 square meters. Based on photographs of the makeshift structure built at the end of the Cultural Revolution, Leshan Hall relied on columns for support and had no walls on any side. If this construction method was similar to the original, then Leshan Hall would not have been suitable for monastic practice; furthermore, its location at the village entrance, where followers tended to gather, rather than in a secluded area suitable for monks, indicates that Leshan Hall did not possess the typical characteristics of a {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=Temple|1=Buddhist temple}}, but rather those of a dining hall. This is one possible reason for the renaming from Longshou Temple to Leshan Hall.
<ref name=":0" />

== Related Activities ==
Every year on the {{ill|Ghost Festival|de|Geisterfest|ja|中元節|zh|中元节|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ghost Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ghost Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}} (the fifteenth day of the seventh month of the {{ill|Lunar Calendar|de|Mondkalender|ja|農暦|zh|农历|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国暦|1=Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134032}}), the Manichaean believers of Shangwan Village gather at Leshan Hall to perform a ritual for the transference of the souls of the deceased. This concept of soul transference is at odds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light, indicating that Manichaeism in Xiapu, in order to become popular and endure locally, had incorporated some unique elements—such as this soul-transference ritual—that provided comfort to the people.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=马小鹤 |author2=吴春明 |title=摩尼教与济度亡灵——霞浦明教《奏申牒书科册》研究 |journal=九州学林 |language=zh |publication-date=2010 |issue=03 |page=45-46}}</ref>


== See Also ==
* {{ill|Xiapu Documents|de|Xiapu-Dokumente|ja|霞浦文書|zh|霞浦文书|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Xiapu Documents|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  A collection of important documents on Manichaeism in Xiapu, it is currently the longest extant collection of Manichaean Chinese texts, with an account of Leshan Hall found in the manuscript "Leshan Hall Divine Record".
* {{ill|Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|de|Manichäische Steinskulpturen aus Shangwan Village|ja|上万村摩尼教石彫造像|zh|上万村摩尼教石雕造像|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Manichaean Stone Carvings of Shangwan Village|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Built near Leshan Hall to adorn the Three Buddha Pagoda behind the tower and the Dengdong Stone Pagoda, it is one of the few surviving examples of Manichaean stone carving art.
* {{ill|Fushou Palace in Puxi|de|Fushou-Palast in Puxi|ja|浦西境福寿宮|zh|浦西境福寿宫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fushou Palace in Puxi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}  
  Located outside the south gate of {{ill|Fuzhou|en|Fuzhou|WD=Q68481|lt=Fuzhou City|1=Fuzhou}}, it is the best-preserved Manichaean palace building, enshrining Lin Deng who once aided in disaster relief in Fuzhou.

{{Not|Longshou Temple}}
{{Coord|27|05|58.6|N|119|54|49.4|E|display=title}}
[[File:泗洲文佛像和乐山堂遗址.jpg|thumb|Photo of the Leshan Hall ruins exhibited at the {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County|1=Xiapu}} Museum]]

'''Leshan Hall''', commonly known as '''Gaizhu Hall''', formerly known as '''Longshou Temple''', is located in China, {{ill|Fujian|en|Fujian|WD=Q41705|lt=Fujian Province|1=Fujian}}, {{ill|Ningde|en|Ningde|WD=Q68919|lt=Ningde City|1=Ningde}}, {{ill|Xiapu|en|Xiapu|WD=Q241877|lt=Xiapu County|1=Xiapu}}, {{ill|Baiyang Township|de|Baiyang Township|ja|柏洋郷|zh|柏洋乡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Baiyang Township|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|de|Tahu-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|塔後村 (霞浦県)|zh|塔后村|lt=Tahu Village|lt_ja=塔後村|lt_de=Tahu-Dorf|lt_zh=塔后村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tahu Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and {{ill|Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|de|Shangwan-Dorf (Xiapu County)|ja|上万村 (霞浦県)|zh|上万村|lt=Shangwan Natural Village|lt_ja=上万自然村|lt_de=Shangwan-Naturdorf|lt_zh=上万自然村|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shangwan Village (Xiapu County)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. It was established by Fujian {{ill|Mingjiao|de|Mingjiao|ja|明教|zh|明教|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mingjiao|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 唐代三夷教|1=Three Persian religions|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11418516}} ({{ill|Manichaeism|en|Manichaeism|WD=Q131165|lt=Manichaeism|1=Manichaeism}}) follower {{ill|Sun Mian|de|Sun Mian|ja|孫綿|zh|孙绵|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sun Mian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} during the {{ill|Northern Song|de|Nördliche Song-Dynastie|ja|北宋|zh|北宋|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Northern Song|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Northern Song (960–1127)|WD=Q319460}} period in the fourth year of {{ill|Qiande (Northern Song)|de|Qiande (Northern Song)|ja|乾德 (北宋)|zh|乾德|lt=Qiande|lt_ja=乾德|lt_de=Qiande|lt_zh=乾德|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiande (Northern Song)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (966 AD), making it the earliest Manichaean building constructed in Fujian. For over a thousand years, it served as an important venue for Manichaean activities in the Baiyang area until it was destroyed in 2006 by {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Sangmei Typhoon|lt_ja=桑美台風|lt_de=Sangmei-Taifun|lt_zh=桑美台风|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}.
<ref name=":0">{{Cite journal |author=樊丽沙 |author2=杨富学 |title=霞浦摩尼教文献及其重要性 |journal=世界宗教研究 |language=zh |publisher=中国社会科学院世界宗教研究所 |publication-place=北京 |publication-date=2011 |issue=06 |issn=1000-4289}}</ref>
<ref name="Kósa2018">{{Cite book|last=Kósa|first=Gábor|title=Der östliche Manichäismus im Spiegel seiner Buch- und Schriftkultur|publisher=Walter de Gruyter GmbH & Co KG|date=2018|isbn=978-3-11-059393-8|language=de}}</ref>

== Name ==
Leshan Hall was originally established as "Longshou Temple" and was renamed "Leshan Hall" during the Yuan dynasty. Initially, the name "Leshan" was believed to be derived from the phrase "The wise delight in water, and the benevolent delight in mountains" found in {{ill|Analects|en|Analects|WD=Q276015|lt=The Analects|1=Analects}} (Lunyu: Yong Ye). Another theory suggests that the term "Leshan" originates from {{ill|Middle Persian|de|Mittelpersisch|ja|中古ペルシア語|zh|中古波斯语|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Middle Persian|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} {{Lang|pal|Rōšan}} (or {{Lang|pal|Rōšn}}), meaning "light", which corresponds with the Manichaean doctrine that venerates light. Moreover, since the building was more likely used as a dining hall for followers rather than as a monastery (see the "Architectural Style and Nature" section), the change from "Temple" (寺) to "Hall" (堂) was probably due to this reason.

In the fifth year of {{ill|Tiansheng|de|Tiansheng|ja|天聖|zh|天圣|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tiansheng|1=Tiansheng|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10939230}} (1027 AD), a resident of Shangwan Village, {{ill|Lin Deng|de|Lin Deng|ja|林瞪|zh|林瞪|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lin Deng|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, converted to Mingjiao at Longshou Temple, later becoming the leading missionary master of Mingjiao in the area and the second abbot of Longshou Temple. His direct disciple, Chen Pingshan, succeeded him.
<ref>{{Cite journal |author=杨富学 |title=林瞪及其在中国摩尼教史上的地位 |journal=中国史研究 |language=zh |publication-place=北京市 |publication-date=2014 |issue=01 |page=109-124 |issn=1002-7963}}</ref>

During the Yuan dynasty, Longshou Temple was renamed Leshan Hall, and during the Ming and Qing dynasties, Leshan Hall underwent two major renovations. In the early years of the People's Republic of China, according to local elders, Leshan Hall still had several courtyards. At the beginning of the {{ill|Cultural Revolution|en|Cultural Revolution|WD=Q8690|lt=Cultural Revolution|1=Cultural Revolution}}, Leshan Hall was demolished and subsequently received only simple repairs with makeshift shed-like structures used for storing villagers' farming tools and for temporary shelter from rain. In 2006, Leshan Hall was destroyed by the {{ill|Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|de|Taifun Sangmei (2006)|ja|颱風桑美 (2006年)|zh|颱風桑美|lt=Super Typhoon Sangmei|lt_ja=超強台風桑美|lt_de=Super-Taifun Sangmei|lt_zh=超强台风桑美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Typhoon Sangmei (2006)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, leaving only the foundation columns, steps, and the base of the shrine.
<ref name=":0" />

== Architectural Style and Nature ==
Due to its great antiquity and the lack of written records, the original appearance of Leshan Hall is unknown. The site covers an area of approximately 1560 square meters. Based on photographs of the makeshift structure built at the end of the Cultural Revolution, Leshan Hall relied on columns for support and had no walls on any side. If this construction method was similar to the original, then Leshan Hall would not have been suitable for monastic practice; furthermore, its location at the village entrance, where followers tended to gather, rather than in a secluded area suitable for monks, indicates that Leshan Hall did not possess the typical characteristics of a {{ill|Buddhist temple|en|Buddhist temple|WD=Q5393308|lt=Temple|1=Buddhist temple}}, but rather those of a dining hall. This is one possible reason for the renaming from Longshou Temple to Leshan Hall.
<ref name=":0" />

== Related Activities ==
Every year on the {{ill|Ghost Festival|de|Geisterfest|ja|中元節|zh|中元节|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ghost Festival|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中元|1=Ghost Festival|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q696781}} (the fifteenth day of the seventh month of the {{ill|Lunar Calendar|de|Mondkalender|ja|農暦|zh|农历|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Lunar Calendar|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 中国暦|1=Chinese calendar|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q134032}}), the Manichaean believers of Shangwan Village gather at Leshan Hall to perform a ritual for the transference of the souls of the deceased.
